item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image is an art installation by Mark Jenkins (as seen here), and the second image could not be sourced. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, although I noticed quite a few errors.

Grammar. SCP Procedures. First line. Reads 'Due the nature of the anomaly…'. Should read 'Due to the nature of the anomaly…'

Grammar. Addendum 1647-1. Third Paragraph. Line two. Reads 'causing to Professors'. Either remove the 'to', or place it after the word 'Professors'.

Grammar. Addendum 1647-1. Third Paragraph. Fourth line. Shouldn't the word 'Respectively' be after the word 'physics'.

Formatting error. Level 3/1647 Clearance Required. Shouldn't this be one of those collapsible links?

Grammar. Level 3/1647 Clearance Required. Third line. '…and the termination of all the possibly involved personnel'. Should be '…and the terminator of all possibly involved personnel'.

Grammar. Level 3/1647 Clearance Required. Fifth line. '…efforts for both execution and covering…' Should be '..efforts for both execution and cover..' or '…efforts for both execution and maintaining cover…'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Timmy hated school. It was all just so boring and stupid. The teachers would take hours to explain every basic concept. Like, it's just integration of multi-variable polynomial expressions! What's next week's lesson? Two plus two equals four! And they would always try to catch him just when he wasn't paying attention, then hand out detention for "doodling" in class. Doodling! It's like they don't even recognize cognitive harmonics expressed in circuit diagram notation!
He'd lost track of how many times he'd been forced to stay after class and clean blackboards.
Hours he could have spent in the garage, perfecting his designs.

But he'd show them! They wanted clean blackboards so badly, they could have them! They'd have the cleanest blackboards in Louisiana!
He'd need to think of something special for Coach Peters. Being forced to run laps really pissed Timmy off. Hey, there's an idea, "pissed"…hmmm.

Timmy's parents made him change schools after the "gas leak". Which was fine by Timmy. The new teachers weren't any better, but he needed time to plan. He hadn't prepared for those government guys to show up. They ruined everything! He couldn't very well take credit for the most genius prank ever when everyone thought it was just toxic fumes from the chem lab! Well, he'd just have to go bigger next time. Let's see them explain away some dead bodies!

This is what happens when peak teen nerd angst collides with the possibilities of an anomalous universe. You get vindictive, petty (but escalating) supervillains-in-development.
Just be glad he was a tech geek, the revenge of the otaku doesn't bear thinking about.

item.title: Scp 1647
item.content:encoded: Just looking at the picture Makes me feel weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a keter, Gettysburg is dumb, and the cause>consequence of the artile is incredibly dumb we are talking angsty super intelligent emo teenagers dumb. The effects are really great but the whole explanation of the scip makes it blow up like Chinese fireworks. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wantto like this scip, I just can't. It is very well written but for me it just stretches the imagination too far.

As others have said - super smart kids who happen to be emotionally damaged enough to create these orbs is unusual, but personally I can go with the flow on that because teenagers are screwy with hormones and can be petty as all hell at the best of times. But the idea that they are only targeting teachers? Not one bully got caught in the crossfire in at least a thousand incidents?

Another issue I have is that these events are happening up to 54 times annually - I might be wrong here, but that suggests to me that this is happening at least once a week, every week, every year, since the early 2000s.

Am I supposed to believe the Foundation - with everything else it has on its plate - is content to just sit there twiddling its figurative thumbs with an MTF and the amnestics on standby, prepared to travel all across the Southern US dispensing amnestics to entire schools (and possibly parents, teachers and the media) at the slightest hint there's a 1647-A active? When the incidents are occurring with that level of frequency? It's too passive, and the Gettysburg proposal just highlights that for me even more. Here is a man who is so sick of dealing with the same scenario over and over that he volunteers genocide as a viable solution. And I can't blame him.

It just goes beyond suspension of belief. It's inconceivable, and unfortunately, not in the good way. That is just my opinion, however. Also, I spotted several mentions of 'SCP-1647's'. Still a typo or intentionally left there?

item.title: Re: Typos?
item.content:encoded: The second one is irony. Gonna fix the other one.

item.title: Typos?
item.content:encoded:
"Two similar notes when then found within other instances of SCP-1647-A from other SCP-1647’s events."

I'm not sure what you were trying to say here. I think you meant,

"Two similar notes were then found within other instances of SCP-1647-A from other SCP-1647 events.

Also, the note itself misspells "Buffoons". Not sure if this is deliberate irony or a typo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, same here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oooh, I get it, that's clever!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was meant affectionately, though in retrospect affection probably seems like a really strange thing coming from me.

Nah, not really. You have really high standards, but I am sure you know how display affection when you want to.

In this particular case, the capabilities of the party behind the skip and the motives just aren't clicking for me. It's a dissonant note in the narrative.

I don't know, but I think that if a group of 16 years olds finds a way to create super-tech mind-affecting orbs, I'm pretty sure they'll use it against the school system, one way or another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hazardous Material Containment Liason Supervisor :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also use it. 173 used it to implicitly mean the individual in charge of that object's containment (as far as you can make out by reading it, anyway), and that's what FFB, myself and a few others take it to mean now. Senior Researcher on an object is head of research of that object; HMCL is head of containment for that object.

Author: I liked it, but it's not a Keter, to my mind. I feel like this is more of an 'inconvenient Euclid.' No vote for the moment, but I may revisit later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty sure Sensei is riffing on your joke. But didn't have to time to reply, because, well, thirteen minutes to Judge Wapner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this directed at me? Because I don't understand the significance, if it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's in the containment procedures for 173, and FortuneFavorsBold uses it in some of his SCPs. Basically, it's a supervisor of some sort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: just curious. What is HMCL?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you an excellent SCPer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Should I be honored?

It was meant affectionately, though in retrospect affection probably seems like a really strange thing coming from me.

Anyway, yeah, all my articles contain almos unrelated themes. I mean, ovoid vehicles and a war between super-evolved Turkeys and Herons, an extraterrestrial device and religious sheeps…this is the most normal one so far.

I don't mind strange or unrelated bits. We're writing about lapses in consensus reality, after all. But even with strange subject material, there's a sort of internal logic that I find is necessary for something to be engaging. In this particular case, the capabilities of the party behind the skip and the motives just aren't clicking for me. It's a dissonant note in the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Riemann*

Although I guess I could be a phonetic Dustin Hoffman.

Edit: He fixed it. Though still, phonetic Dustin Hoffman.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. There are some Euclid skips where the basic anomaly is uncontainable, like SCP-1656.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (EDIT):Even though it is uncontainable I still think its doesn't quite merit Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you upvote it? Cause I don't see your vote in the rating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you add more background to the spheres? Also I like this +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the actual anomaly is still uncontainable, I still hold that it's Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One request. Please get rid of the marked out extranormal classification please. Safe Eucild Keter work fine. /Never mind I see why it's extranormal due to be seen as a odd event at first and nice writing idea for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the raison d'être you've come up with is a bit bland, but otherwise the skip is pretty competent in my opinion. There are a lot of little word choice quibbles (like your use of the word "enabled" instead of "implemented" or "enacted"), but it kept my interest until the end.

No vote, because the hook is weak, but otherwise it's alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Please don't link the images to your sandbox page, but upload them to the page itself.

As for the article, it's far too convoluted for my tastes. I liked the image of teachers licking the blackboard though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is way longer than the content warrants.

There's no hook.

I lost interest after the sphere thing was introduced, because the "explanation" of the sphere breaks down to angry malevolent force.

I would like this if you started over with the concept of crazy teachers as a skip and not a half finished mystery story. I think you need to explore the core concept more, because everything else seems tacked on.

The tone is promising, but you use some empathic words here and there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that case I'll defer the issue to the more experienced writer, I was just voicing my thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed some minor grammatical stuff and localization issues per te.

per te.

Should I be honored?

Anyway, yeah, all my articles contain almos unrelated themes. I mean, ovoid vehicles and a war between super-evolved Turkeys and Herons, an extraterrestrial device and religious sheeps…this is the most normal one so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was Euclid at first, but Azzleflux said that this was more like a Keter, so…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure thing c:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the idea of school shootings being foundation work, but that's gonna raise some eyebrows.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pictures alone got me to upvote. But really this was great. Really unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I like it!

One thing bugs me, though. The spheres are stated as being positively identified as the cause of the events, but there's no true explanation of how this was determined. If it was determined when the Foundation researchers went crazy, you should make a specific reference to that fact.

Also, you might want to put that last bit in a collapsible, just to emphasize the whole "you're not supposed to read this" thing. Just my two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally like this. It could due with a bit of rewriting and I could have swore this was a -J at first, though given the majority of the second half of the article made it worthy of being a mainlister.

Not all mainlisters have to be serious (999, in example) and, given some rewiting, you could appeal to the regulars and bring in the upvotes!

Good luck, and take my upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Following this event, SCP-1647 has occurred ███ times. SCP-1647 has been classified Keter as of 199█/██/██.

Wait, so this event happened over a hundred times before the Foundation thought about classifying it to Keter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must mention that the kids that could pull this off would probably have a 130 IQ and higher and certain personality issues, so smart that they find everything school related boring and bland. However, I must admit the entire thing is perhaps a bit convoluted. Will keep the article close so I read any changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I'll second this. This thought definitely ran through my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it has all the problems of Kalinin's suggested decomm, plus some others. I'm inclined to agree about the major, major scale mismatch here. We're looking at a person or organization that drops supertech mind control spheres into schools across a massive geographic area every few days, somehow untrackably, and they're doing all this to express their dissatisfaction with American educators? Even given that motive, it's a lousy way to go about it.

Also, amnesticizing whole schools is a pretty visible coverup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally don't see how this is Keter. While it is uncontainable, the effects are relatively harmless (only resulting in property damage and, at the most, one death) and can be mitigated with a quick response team and some amnestics. Since nobody else has mentioned it it's probably just a case of me over-thinking things, but I'd just like to put my opinion out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's interesting, because while reading this, I was going, "This anomaly is known as 'being a teacher in the United States'" and then you actually took the current events route. I like it.

This does need some proofreading, though. Word use issues like "enable" and unnecessary prepositions abound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has some grammar and usage issues. May I fix them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed some minor grammatical stuff and localization issues per te.

As for the article…ehhhh, I'm not really sold on this "revenge on the teachers" thing as a concept, but even if I was, the execution is kind of bogged down here.

For starters, I know it shows up in other articles, but I don't care for the repeatedly struck through object classes. Seems to me that changes in classification would be covered in the note that you included later in the article. From an aesthetic standpoint, I find it displeasing. The containment drags a bit, making it feel like I've had to slog before I've even gotten to the skip, which has the effect, in my opinion, of burying your lede.

The sphere-thing being put into the teacher along with a note…I dunno. It's not egregious, but it seems a tad over the top in terms of inserting scary imagery. It also further cements in my mind a kind of silly image of the creator of this phenomenon. You've got someone or something sophisticated enough to make anti-grav mind-warping orbs and perfrom complex human surgery, but its stated goal is "school sucks maaaan". Kind of a disconnect for me.

Finally, speaking as someone who got skewered recently for even hinting at a complicated decomm attempt, the very end of the article strikes me as unnecessarily distracting from your skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum 1647-4 has an SCP-XXXX in it. Might want to fix that.

Gettysburg seems really excessive. While a keter, this skip is relatively easy to contain and also relatively not dangerous - killing everyone possibly affected would make the situation worse AND be harder to cover up. I don't see why didn't the Ethics commitee laugh at the guy who proposed it before rejecting it outright, rather than politely going "maybe later". I think that section makes the article worse, not better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's a Keter this time. With humanoids and spheres. Thanks Azzleflux, Soulless Singularity and Riemann for helping me. Plus I sent a PM to Smapti but idk. Will fix the pictures later. It's a bit late now.
